title: The War of Endings: Ceasefires Test Two Fronts as Peace Takes Shape
subhead: Iran responds to US ceasefire proposals while Putin declares Ukraine 'coming to an end' — but both conflicts face spoilers, violations, and critical unresolved issues that could unravel diplomatic progress.

sections.heading: Iran Responds to US Ceasefire Framework
sections.body:
Iran has formally responded to the latest US ceasefire proposal via Pakistani mediators, signaling its willingness to negotiate a permanent end to the war on all fronts. The response, delivered Sunday, focuses on ending the conflict comprehensively — including the Lebanon front where Israel continues to battle Hezbollah — and ensuring maritime security in the Strait of Hormuz.

The US proposal reportedly centers on a 14-point memorandum addressing nuclear enrichment suspension, sanctions relief, and restoring free transit through the strategic waterway. However, significant gaps remain. Iran prefers to discuss its nuclear program later, while Israel insists that Iran's stockpile of highly enriched uranium — over 440 kilograms at up to 60% purity — must be 'taken out' before any war-ending agreement.

The fragile ceasefire was tested Sunday when drones targeted shipping off Qatar and entered airspace over Kuwait and the UAE. Qatar called the attacks a 'dangerous and unacceptable escalation,' while the UAE blamed Iran for two drones it shot down. No casualties were reported, but the incidents underscore how quickly the situation could deteriorate.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's response marks the most serious diplomatic opening since the war began on February 28. Pakistan's role as mediator continues to prove crucial, and the fact that Tehran is engaging on comprehensive terms suggests both sides may be looking for an exit. But Israel's demand regarding enriched uranium, Iran's insistence on maritime control, and the risk of spoilers on all sides mean the path to peace remains treacherous.

sections.heading: Putin Declares Ukraine War 'Coming to an End'
sections.body:
After a scaled-back Victory Day parade in Moscow that featured no tanks, missiles, or military hardware for the first time in nearly two decades, Russian President Vladimir Putin declared that the Ukraine conflict is 'coming to an end.' Speaking to reporters Saturday, he said he saw potential for negotiations while condemning Western support for Kyiv.

The parade itself was a stark symbol of Russia's changed circumstances. Authorities cited fears of Ukrainian drone attacks for the reduced spectacle, with military hardware displayed only on giant screens rather than rolling through Red Square. Fewer international journalists were granted access, and fewer world leaders attended.

Despite Putin's optimistic framing, the three-day US-brokered ceasefire has already frayed. Both Moscow and Kyiv accused each other of hundreds of violations, with Ukraine reporting casualties from Russian drone and artillery strikes and Russia claiming over 1,000 ceasefire breaches by Ukrainian forces. A planned prisoner exchange of 1,000 from each side has not yet materialized.
sections.whyItMatters: Putin's rhetoric suggests Moscow may be positioning for negotiations, but the reality on the ground tells a different story. The stripped-down Victory Day parade — normally a showcase of Russian military power — reveals Kremlin anxiety about Ukrainian capabilities. Russia's demand that Ukraine withdraw from Donbas before any settlement remains unchanged, and Kyiv shows no sign of accepting territorial losses. The war may be 'ending' in Putin's narrative, but the fighting continues.

sections.heading: Lebanon Ceasefire in Name Only
sections.body:
Despite a US-announced ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon on April 16, intense fighting continues along the border. Lebanese health authorities report 39 people killed in Israeli strikes on Sunday alone, with more than 120 killed in the past week. The ministry does not distinguish between combatants and civilians.

Israel says it is targeting Hezbollah infrastructure and operatives, and acknowledges reports of civilian harm. One strike on the southern town of Saksakiyeh killed seven people including a child; another reportedly targeted a Syrian father and his 12-year-old daughter on a motorcycle, with the drone striking three times. The girl was undergoing life-saving surgery.

Hezbollah continues to launch rockets and drones into northern Israel, wounding three Israeli reservists in one attack. Israel maintains a 10-kilometer occupation zone in southern Lebanon, where entire villages have been destroyed in operations that rights groups say could amount to war crimes. Over 2,700 people have been killed in Lebanon since March 2.
sections.whyItMatters: The Lebanon front has become a forgotten war within the larger Iran conflict. While diplomacy focuses on Hormuz and nuclear issues, Hezbollah and Israel fight on. Any comprehensive peace deal must address Lebanon, but neither side shows signs of de-escalation. The humanitarian toll mounts daily, and the destruction of border communities will shape regional politics for years.

sections.heading: Saudi Aramco Profits Surge as Pipelines Bypass Hormuz
sections.body:
Saudi oil giant Aramco reported a 25% jump in first-quarter profits to $32.5 billion, crediting its ability to reroute exports through the East-West Pipeline that runs across Saudi Arabia to the Red Sea. The pipeline is now operating at maximum capacity of 7 million barrels per day, helping mitigate the disruption to global oil supplies caused by Iran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

However, the pipeline cannot fully compensate for Hormuz's capacity. Before the war, roughly 20% of globally traded oil flowed through the strait daily, along with significant natural gas and fertilizer shipments. The closure continues to exert upward pressure on global energy prices and supply chains.

Aramco's CEO described recent events as a 'stark reminder that reliable energy supply is critical,' highlighting the company's efforts to navigate the geopolitical disruption through infrastructure investments and operational flexibility.
sections.whyItMatters: The energy market is adapting to the new reality of Hormuz disruption, but at a cost. Saudi Arabia's pipeline capacity provides crucial relief, but it's not enough to fully offset the lost shipping lanes. Higher oil prices ripple through the global economy, contributing to inflation and economic pressure. The war's economic effects extend far beyond the battlefield.

sections.heading: Hungary's Democratic Transition
sections.body:
Hungary's new Prime Minister Péter Magyar was sworn in Saturday after his Tisza party's landslide victory ended 16 years of Viktor Orbán's rule. Tisza holds 141 of 199 parliamentary seats — up from zero when the party was founded just two years ago. Orbán's Fidesz party collapsed from 135 seats to 52.

Magyar pledged to 'serve, not rule' Hungary and promised systemic change beyond mere government turnover. His incoming government faces grim economic conditions after years of state contracts flowing to Fidesz-connected business circles. The budget deficit has already swollen to planned full-year targets. New ministers are establishing an 'office to recover stolen assets' while promising rule-of-law accountability rather than political revenge.

The European Commission had withheld €17 billion in funds from the Orbán government over rule-of-law concerns. Magyar now seeks to repair relations with Brussels while navigating difficult questions about EU migrant policy and Ukraine's potential accession — issues where Hungarian public opinion diverges from EU consensus.
sections.whyItMatters: Hungary's political earthquake is a significant shift in European politics. Orbán's illiberal model had been a template for nationalist movements across the continent; its rejection by Hungarian voters sends a powerful signal. But Magyar faces steep challenges: economic crisis, the need to recover allegedly stolen assets, and pressure from both Brussels and Moscow. His success or failure will shape Europe's political trajectory.

sections.heading: Pakistan Terror Attack Kills 15 Police
sections.body:
A suicide car bombing killed 15 Pakistani police officers at a checkpoint in Bannu, in the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province bordering Afghanistan. A vehicle loaded with explosives rammed the guard post late Saturday, after which the attacker entered an adjoining building and fired on remaining officers. Backup units arriving at the scene also came under fire.

The alliance Ittehad-ul-Mujahideen, linked to the Pakistani Taliban, claimed responsibility. The group formed in April 2025 from the merger of several militant factions with a history of attacks in the region. Three officers were pulled alive from the rubble; the checkpoint was reduced to debris.

Pakistan continues to face violence from multiple armed groups, including separatists in Balochistan and Islamists in the northwest. The government blames Afghanistan for harboring militants who plot attacks from across the border — an accusation the Afghan Taliban denies.
sections.whyItMatters: Pakistan's role as mediator in the Iran-US conflict gives it geopolitical significance, but its internal security challenges are intensifying. As Islamabad helps broker peace between major powers, it struggles to contain militants within its own borders. The contradiction highlights how regional powers can be both peacemakers and conflict zones simultaneously.

bottomLine: This weekend marked a potential inflection point in two major wars. Iran's formal response to US ceasefire proposals and Putin's declaration that Ukraine is 'coming to an end' suggest both conflicts may be entering endgame phases. But the gaps between rhetoric and reality remain vast. Israel demands Iran's enriched uranium be removed; Russia demands Ukraine withdraw from Donbas. Both face determined adversaries unwilling to accept maximalist terms. Meanwhile, the Lebanon front burns largely unnoticed, energy markets strain under Hormuz disruption, and Hungary's political revolution tests whether democratic renewal can succeed in an era of populist decline. The coming days will reveal whether the language of 'ending' translates into actual peace, or whether the wars grind on toward new chapters of violence.
